title: Female employment rate, 1980 vs. 2024
subtitle: Share of the working-age population who are employed. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries.
caption: Across a global sample of countries, the share of the working-age female population in employment has generally risen between 1980 and 2024, with the magnitude of change varying significantly by region. The scatter plot places 1980 rates on the vertical axis and 2024 rates on the horizontal axis, both scaled from 0% to 100%, with circle size indicating total population. Most data points cluster below the diagonal line that would represent no change, visually confirming that 2024 values exceed 1980 values for the majority of nations shown. The largest gains appear in countries that started with low participation; Bangladesh and Pakistan, both represented by large teal circles, sit near the bottom of the 1980 axis at roughly 10% but have moved rightward to the 30% to 40% range in 2024. Similarly, the United Arab Emirates shows a shift from near 0% in 1980 to approximately 50% in 2024. In contrast, nations with already high participation in 1980 show more modest horizontal movement. Thailand and Burkina Faso occupy the upper portion of the chart, with 1980 rates between 70% and 80%, and 2024 rates extending slightly further right toward 60% to 70%. The United States and Brazil, marked by large orange and brown circles respectively, display 1980 rates around 45% to 50% and 2024 rates near 55% to 60%. European nations like Italy, Spain, and the Netherlands cluster in the middle, with 1980 rates between 25% and 40% and 2024 rates generally falling between 50% and 60%. At the extreme right, the Bahamas and Nigeria show 2024 rates approaching or exceeding 80%, while Malawi remains an outlier with a high 1980 rate near 90% but a lower 2024 value around 35%. The distribution shows a clear pattern of convergence, where countries with lower initial female employment rates in 1980 have experienced the largest absolute increases by 2024.
